verses.commentary: The collection for Jerusalem's poor unites Gentile churches in tangible solidarity. Paul instructs Corinth to follow the pattern given to Galatian churches. Consistency across regions demonstrates unity.
On the first day of every week, each should set aside money proportional to income. Systematic giving prevents last-minute scrambling. Regular discipline turns generosity into spiritual rhythm.
When Paul arrives, he'll send approved representatives with letters to deliver the gift to Jerusalem. Accountability and transparency protect integrity. Multiple witnesses guard against accusations.
If it's advisable for Paul to go, the representatives will accompany him. Flexibility based on circumstances. Plans serve mission, not ego.
Paul will visit Corinth after passing through Macedonia. Travel plans shape ministry schedule. Geography determines sequence of engagement.
He may stay with them awhile, perhaps through winter, so they can help him on his journey. Extended visits deepen relationships. Their support enables continued mission.
Paul doesn't want a passing visit. He hopes to spend time with them, if the Lord permits. Quality over brevity. Divine sovereignty governs all plans.
He'll stay in Ephesus until Pentecost. Strategic timing maximizes ministry impact. Current opportunities take precedence over future possibilities.
A great door for effective work has opened, though many oppose him. Opposition doesn't negate opportunity. Resistance confirms significance of gospel advance.
When Timothy comes, receive him without fear. He carries on the Lord's work just as Paul does. Timothy's youth doesn't diminish his authority.
No one should treat him with contempt. Send him on in peace so he may return to Paul. Respect and support for those in ministry honors their calling.
Paul strongly urged Apollos to visit with the brothers, but Apollos was unwilling now. He'll go when opportunity arises. Ministry timing involves personal readiness, not just external invitation.
Be alert, stand firm in the faith, be courageous, be strong. Four imperatives: vigilance, stability, bravery, strength. Spiritual warfare demands active resistance.
Do everything in love. Love governs all actions. The supreme ethic qualifies every command. Without love, obedience becomes legalism.
Stephanas's household were the first converts in Achaia, devoted to serving the saints. Firstfruits carry special honor. Devoted service merits recognition.
Submit to such people and everyone who joins in the work and labors. Honor flows to those who serve sacrificially. Submission recognizes godly leadership.
Paul rejoices at the arrival of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus. They supplied what was lacking from the Corinthians' absence. Representation bridges physical distance.
They refreshed Paul's spirit and the Corinthians' as well. Such people deserve recognition. Ministry partnership encourages both sender and receiver.
The churches in Asia send greetings. Aquila and Priscilla greet warmly, along with the church meeting in their house. House churches were the norm. Personal homes served corporate mission.
All the brothers and sisters send greetings. Greet one another with a holy kiss. Physical affection expressed spiritual kinship. Cultural practice carried theological significance.
Paul writes this greeting in his own hand. Personal authentication distinguished genuine correspondence from forgery. Handwritten closings verified apostolic authorship.
If anyone doesn't love the Lord, let them be cursed. Come, Lord! Love for Christ defines genuine faith. Anathema for those who reject Him. Maranatha: eschatological longing for Christ's return.
The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you. Standard Pauline benediction. Grace bookends the letter, beginning and ending with divine favor.
My love to all of you in Christ Jesus. Amen. Despite rebukes, Paul's love remains constant. Correction flows from affection. Apostolic authority serves pastoral care.
